heading: 9.Instruction Set (part 97)
content:
The integer `wgmma.mma_async` operation is performed with `.s32` accumulators. The
  `.satfinite` qualifier indicates that on overflow, the accumulated value is limited to the
  range *MIN_INT32*.. *MAX_INT32* (where the bounds are defined as the minimum negative signed
  32-bit integer and the maximum positive signed 32-bit integer respectively).

  If `.satfinite` is not specified, the accumulated value is wrapped instead.

Note

For `.atype` and `.btype` as `.e4m3` or `.e5m2` with `.dtype` as `.f32`, current
implementation does accumulation at higher than half precision but lower than single
precision.

The mandatory `.sync` qualifier indicates that `wgmma.mma_async` instruction causes the
executing thread to wait until all threads in the warp execute the same `wgmma.mma_async`
instruction before resuming execution.

The mandatory `.aligned` qualifier indicates that all threads in the warpgroup must execute the
same `wgmma.mma_async` instruction. In conditionally executed code, a `wgmma.mma_async`
instruction should only be used if it is known that all threads in the warpgroup evaluate the
condition identically, otherwise behavior is undefined.

PTX ISA Notes

Introduced in PTX ISA version 8.0.

Support for `.u8.s8` and `.s8.u8` as .atype.btype introduced in PTX ISA version 8.4.

Target ISA Notes

Requires `sm_90a`.

Examples of half precision floating point type

```
.reg .f16x2 f16a<40>, f16d<40>;
.reg .f32   f32d<40>;
.reg .b64   descA, descB;
.reg .pred  scaleD;
wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k16.f32.f16.f16
  {f32d0, f32d1, f32d2, f32d3},
  {f16a0, f16a1, f16a2, f16a3},
  descB,
  1, -1, -1, 1;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n72k16.f16.f16.f16
  {f16d0, f16d1,  f16d2,  f16d3,  f16d4,  f16d5,  f16d6,  f16d7,  f16d8,
   f16d9, f16d10, f16d11, f16d12, f16d13, f16d14, f16d15, f16d16, f16d17},
  descA,
  descB,
  scaleD, -1, 1, 1, 0;
```

Examples of alternate floating point type

```
.reg .f32   f32d<40>;
.reg .b32   bf16a<40>
.reg .b64   descA, descB;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n120k16.f32.bf16.bf16
  {f32d0, f32d1, f32d2, f32d3, f32d4, f32d5, f32d6, f32d7, f32d8, f32d9,
   f32d10, f32d11, f32d12, f32d13, f32d14, f32d15, f32d16, f32d17, f32d18, f32d19,
   f32d20, f32d21, f32d22, f32d23, f32d24, f32d25, f32d26, f32d27, f32d28, f32d29,
   f32d30, f32d31, f32d32, f32d33, f32d34, f32d35, f32d36, f32d37, f32d38, f32d39,
   f32d40, f32d41, f32d42, f32d43, f32d44, f32d45, f32d46, f32d47, f32d48, f32d49,
   f32d50, f32d51, f32d52, f32d53, f32d54, f32d55, f32d56, f32d57, f32d58, f32d59},
  {bf16a0, bf16a1, bf16a2, bf16a3},
  descB,
  scaleD, -1, -1, 0;

.reg .f32   f32d<40>;
.reg .b64   descA, descB;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n16k8.f32.tf32.tf32
  {f32d0, f32d1, f32d2, f32d3, f32d4, f32d5, f32d6, f32d7},
  descA,
  descB,
  0, -1, -1;

.reg .b32 f16d<8>, f16a<8>;
.reg .f32 f32d<8>;
.reg .b64   descA, descB;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.f16.e4m3.e5m2
  {f16d0, f16d1},
  descA,
  descB,
  scaleD, -1, 1;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.f32.e5m2.e4m3
  {f32d0, f32d1, f32d2, f32d3},
  {f16a0, f16a1, f16a2, f16a3},
  descB,
  1, -1, -1;
```

Examples of integer type

```
.reg .s32 s32d<8>, s32a<8>;
.reg .u32 u32a<8>;
.reg .pred scaleD;
.reg .b64   descA, descB;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.s32.s8.s8.satfinite
  {s32d0, s32d1, s32d2, s32d3},
  {s32a0, s32a1, s32a2, s32a3},
  descB,
  1;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.s32.u8.u8
  {s32d0, s32d1, s32d2, s32d3},
  descA,
  descB,
  scaleD;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.s32.s8.u8.satfinite
  {s32d0, s32d1, s32d2, s32d3},
  {s32a0, s32a1, s32a2, s32a3},
  descB,
  scaleD;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k32.s32.u8.s8
  {s32d0, s32d1, s32d2, s32d3},
  descA,
  descB,
  scaleD;
```

Examples of single bit type

```
.reg .s32 s32d<4>;
.reg .b32 b32a<4>;
.reg .pred scaleD;
.reg .b64   descA, descB;

wgmma.mma_async.sync.aligned.m64n8k256.s32.b1.b1.and.popc
  {s32d0, s32d1, s32d2, s32d3},
  {b32a0, b32a1, b32a2, b32a3},
  descB,
  scaleD;
```

#### 9.7.15.6. [Asynchronous Warpgroup Level Multiply-and-Accumulate Operation using `wgmma.mma_async.sp` instruction](#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-matrix-instructions-for-sparse-wgmma)

This section describes warp-level `wgmma.mma_async.sp` instruction with sparse matrix A. This
variant of the `wgmma.mma_async` operation can be used when A is a structured sparse matrix with
50% zeros in each row distributed in a shape-specific granularity. For an `MxNxK` sparse
`wgmma.mma_async.sp` operation, the `MxK` matrix A is packed into `MxK/2` elements. For each
K-wide row of matrix A, 50% elements are zeros and the remaining `K/2` non-zero elements are
packed in the operand representing matrix A. The mapping of these `K/2` elements to the
corresponding K-wide row is provided explicitly as metadata.

##### 9.7.15.6.1. [Sparse matrix storage](#asynchronous-warpgroup-level-sparse-matrix-storage)

Granularity of sparse matrix A is defined as the ratio of the number of non-zero elements in a
sub-chunk of the matrix row to the total number of elements in that sub-chunk where the size of the
sub-chunk is shape-specific. For example, in a `64x32` matrix A used in floating point
`wgmma.mma_async` operations, sparsity is expected to be at 2:4 granularity, i.e. each 4-element
vector (i.e. a sub-chunk of 4 consecutive elements) of a matrix row contains 2 zeros. Index of each
non-zero element in a sub-chunk is stored in the metadata operand. Values `0b0000`, `0b0101`,
`0b1010`, `0b1111` are invalid values for metadata and will result in undefined behavior. In a
group of four consecutive threads, one or more threads store the metadata for the whole group
depending upon the matrix shape. These threads are specified using an additional sparsity selector operand.

Matrix A and its corresponding input operand to the sparse wgmma is similar to the diagram shown in
[Figure 111](#sparse-mma-storage-example), with an appropriate matrix size.

Granularities for different matrix shapes and data types are described below.

Sparse `wgmma.mma_async.sp` with half-precision and `.bf16` type

For `.f16` and `.bf16` types, for all supported `64xNx32` shapes, matrix A is structured
sparse at a granularity of 2:4. In other words, each chunk of four adjacent elements in a row of
matrix A have two zeroes and two non-zero elements. Only the two non-zero elements are stored in
matrix A and their positions in the four-wide chunk in Matrix A are indicated by two 2-bits indices
in the metadata operand.





[Figure: f16-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .f16/.bf16 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the relationship between a four-wide chunk from a row in matrix A, the indices of elements within that chunk, and how the sparse matrix operand and metadata are derived from the 2:4 structured sparsity pattern.

SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
This is a 64-bit wide register showing 4 elements (each 16 bits wide for f16/bf16):
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: y  (non-zero element)
  Bits 47-32: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (non-zero element)

The pattern is: y, 0, 0, x — meaning elements at positions 3 and 0 are non-zero; elements at positions 2 and 1 are zero. This is a valid 2:4 sparsity pattern (2 non-zeros out of every 4 elements).

SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
This is a 64-bit wide field showing the 2-bit index of each of the 4 elements:
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: 11  (index 3, corresponding to element y)
  Bits 47-32: 10  (index 2, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 01  (index 1, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  00  (index 0, corresponding to element x)

Each element in the four-wide chunk is assigned a 2-bit index: 00=position 0, 01=position 1, 10=position 2, 11=position 3.

SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
This is a 32-bit wide register holding only the 2 non-zero elements (compressed):
Bit positions: 31 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 31-16: y  (the non-zero element from position 3)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (the non-zero element from position 0)

The sparse operand contains only the non-zero values packed together, discarding the zeros.

SECTION 4: METADATA
This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the positions of the two non-zero elements:
Bit positions: 3 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index of the high non-zero element, y, which is at position 3)
  Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index of the low non-zero element, x, which is at position 0)

METADATA ENCODING RULES:
- Each pair of non-zero elements selected from a group of 4 requires 4 bits of metadata total.
- The lower 2 bits (bits 1-0) encode the index (0–3) of the first (lower) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- The upper 2 bits (bits 3-2) encode the index (0–3) of the second (higher) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- In this example: lower non-zero x is at index 00 (position 0), upper non-zero y is at index 11 (position 3).
- Metadata value = 0b1100 = 0xC (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00).

RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY:
Original 4-element chunk: [y, 0, 0, x] at indices [3, 2, 1, 0]
Non-zero elements selected: x (index 0), y (index 3)
Sparse operand (32-bit): [y | x] — y in upper 16 bits, x in lower 16 bits
Metadata (4-bit): [11 | 00] — upper 2 bits = index of y (3), lower 2 bits = index of x (0)

SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .f16/.bf16 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates the relationship between a four-wide chunk from a row in matrix A, the indices of elements within that chunk, and how the sparse matrix operand and metadata are derived from the 2:4 structured sparsity pattern.

SECTION 1: FOUR-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
This is a 64-bit wide register showing 4 elements (each 16 bits wide for f16/bf16):
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: y  (non-zero element)
  Bits 47-32: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 0  (zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (non-zero element)

The pattern is: y, 0, 0, x — meaning elements at positions 3 and 0 are non-zero; elements at positions 2 and 1 are zero. This is a valid 2:4 sparsity pattern (2 non-zeros out of every 4 elements).

SECTION 2: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE FOUR-WIDE CHUNK
This is a 64-bit wide field showing the 2-bit index of each of the 4 elements:
Bit positions: 63 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 63-48: 11  (index 3, corresponding to element y)
  Bits 47-32: 10  (index 2, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 31-16: 01  (index 1, corresponding to zero element)
  Bits 15-0:  00  (index 0, corresponding to element x)

Each element in the four-wide chunk is assigned a 2-bit index: 00=position 0, 01=position 1, 10=position 2, 11=position 3.

SECTION 3: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
This is a 32-bit wide register holding only the 2 non-zero elements (compressed):
Bit positions: 31 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 31-16: y  (the non-zero element from position 3)
  Bits 15-0:  x  (the non-zero element from position 0)

The sparse operand contains only the non-zero values packed together, discarding the zeros.

SECTION 4: METADATA
This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0) encoding the positions of the two non-zero elements:
Bit positions: 3 down to 0
Layout (left to right, high bits to low bits):
  Bits 3-2: 11  (2-bit index of the high non-zero element, y, which is at position 3)
  Bits 1-0: 00  (2-bit index of the low non-zero element, x, which is at position 0)

METADATA ENCODING RULES:
- Each pair of non-zero elements selected from a group of 4 requires 4 bits of metadata total.
- The lower 2 bits (bits 1-0) encode the index (0–3) of the first (lower) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- The upper 2 bits (bits 3-2) encode the index (0–3) of the second (higher) non-zero element within the four-wide chunk.
- In this example: lower non-zero x is at index 00 (position 0), upper non-zero y is at index 11 (position 3).
- Metadata value = 0b1100 = 0xC (bits 3:2 = 11, bits 1:0 = 00).

RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY:
Original 4-element chunk: [y, 0, 0, x] at indices [3, 2, 1, 0]
Non-zero elements selected: x (index 0), y (index 3)
Sparse operand (32-bit): [y | x] — y in upper 16 bits, x in lower 16 bits
Metadata (4-bit): [11 | 00] — upper 2 bits = index of y (3), lower 2 bits = index of x (0)


Figure 171 Sparse WGMMA metadata example for `.f16`/`.bf16` type.

The sparsity selector indicates a thread-pair within a group of four consecutive threads which
contributes the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or
1 (threads T2, T3); any other value results in an undefined behavior.

Sparse `wgmma.mma_async.sp` with `.tf32` type

For `.tf32` type, for all supported `64xNx16` shapes, matrix A is structured sparse at a
granularity of 1:2. In other words, each chunk of two adjacent elements in a row of matrix A have
one zero and one non-zero element. Only the non-zero element is stored in operand for matrix A and
the 4-bit index in the metadata indicates the position of the non-zero element in the two-wide
chunk. 0b1110 and 0b0100 are the only meaningful values of the index, the remaining values result in
an undefined behavior.





[Figure: tf32-metadata-example]
SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .tf32 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how a two-wide chunk from matrix A is compressed into a sparse matrix operand, and how the metadata encodes which elements are selected. The data type is .tf32.

============================================================
SECTION 1: TWO-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide register (bits 63 down to 0), split into two 32-bit halves:

Bit positions:  63 .............. 32 | 31 .............. 0
Contents:              x             |         0

- Bits [63:32]: labeled "x" (non-zero value, kept)
- Bits [31:0]:  labeled "0" (zero value, pruned/discarded)

This represents a 2:4 structured sparsity pattern where 1 out of 2 elements is non-zero (for tf32, the chunk is 2-wide, meaning 2 elements per group, and 1 is selected).

============================================================
SECTION 2: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 32-bit wide register (bits 31 down to 0):

Bit positions:  31 .............. 0
Contents:              x

- Bits [31:0]: labeled "x" (the single non-zero element retained from the two-wide chunk)

The sparse operand is half the width of the original chunk because only the non-zero elements are stored.

============================================================
SECTION 3: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE TWO-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide field (bits 63 down to 0), divided into four 16-bit segments, each holding a 2-bit index value:

Bit positions:  63 .... 48 | 47 .... 32 | 31 .... 16 | 15 .... 0
Index values:      11      |     10      |     01      |    00

- Bits [63:48]: index value = 11 (binary)
- Bits [47:32]: index value = 10 (binary)
- Bits [31:16]: index value = 01 (binary)
- Bits [15:0]:  index value = 00 (binary)

These are the 2-bit indices indicating which element within each two-wide chunk is the non-zero element. Each 2-bit field encodes the column position (0 or 1) of the selected non-zero element within its respective 2-element group.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0), divided into two 2-bit segments:

Bit positions:  3 .... 2 | 1 .... 0
Values:            11    |    10

- Bits [3:2]: metadata value = 11 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the upper (second) group
- Bits [1:0]: metadata value = 10 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the lower (first) group

The metadata register is 4 bits total, encoding two 2-bit selector fields.

============================================================
ENCODING RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY
============================================================

For .tf32 type, each element is 32 bits wide. The two-wide chunk is 64 bits (2 x 32-bit tf32 elements). The sparse operand retains only the non-zero element (32 bits). The metadata uses 2 bits per element group to indicate which of the 2 positions in the chunk holds the non-zero value.

Metadata bit encoding per 2-bit field:
- 00 = element at position 0 (bits [31:0] of chunk) is selected
- 01 = element at position 1 (bits [63:32] of chunk) is selected
- 10 = element at position 2 (used in wider chunks; for 2-wide, encodes position)
- 11 = element at position 3 (used in wider chunks)

In this specific example:
- Lower metadata field [1:0] = 10: selects position 2 within its group
- Upper metadata field [3:2] = 11: selects position 3 within its group

The "Indices of elements within the two-wide chunk" (64-bit field with values 11, 10, 01, 00) shows the full index mapping across all groups, while the actual metadata stored is the compressed 4-bit version (bits 3:0) containing only the two relevant 2-bit selectors (11 and 10) corresponding to the non-zero element positions.

SPARSE MMA METADATA EXAMPLE FOR .tf32 TYPE

OVERVIEW:
This figure illustrates how a two-wide chunk from matrix A is compressed into a sparse matrix operand, and how the metadata encodes which elements are selected. The data type is .tf32.

============================================================
SECTION 1: TWO-WIDE CHUNK FROM A ROW IN MATRIX A
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide register (bits 63 down to 0), split into two 32-bit halves:

Bit positions:  63 .............. 32 | 31 .............. 0
Contents:              x             |         0

- Bits [63:32]: labeled "x" (non-zero value, kept)
- Bits [31:0]:  labeled "0" (zero value, pruned/discarded)

This represents a 2:4 structured sparsity pattern where 1 out of 2 elements is non-zero (for tf32, the chunk is 2-wide, meaning 2 elements per group, and 1 is selected).

============================================================
SECTION 2: SPARSE MATRIX OPERAND
============================================================

This is a 32-bit wide register (bits 31 down to 0):

Bit positions:  31 .............. 0
Contents:              x

- Bits [31:0]: labeled "x" (the single non-zero element retained from the two-wide chunk)

The sparse operand is half the width of the original chunk because only the non-zero elements are stored.

============================================================
SECTION 3: INDICES OF ELEMENTS WITHIN THE TWO-WIDE CHUNK
============================================================

This is a 64-bit wide field (bits 63 down to 0), divided into four 16-bit segments, each holding a 2-bit index value:

Bit positions:  63 .... 48 | 47 .... 32 | 31 .... 16 | 15 .... 0
Index values:      11      |     10      |     01      |    00

- Bits [63:48]: index value = 11 (binary)
- Bits [47:32]: index value = 10 (binary)
- Bits [31:16]: index value = 01 (binary)
- Bits [15:0]:  index value = 00 (binary)

These are the 2-bit indices indicating which element within each two-wide chunk is the non-zero element. Each 2-bit field encodes the column position (0 or 1) of the selected non-zero element within its respective 2-element group.

============================================================
SECTION 4: METADATA
============================================================

This is a 4-bit wide field (bits 3 down to 0), divided into two 2-bit segments:

Bit positions:  3 .... 2 | 1 .... 0
Values:            11    |    10

- Bits [3:2]: metadata value = 11 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the upper (second) group
- Bits [1:0]: metadata value = 10 (binary) — corresponds to the index of the non-zero element in the lower (first) group

The metadata register is 4 bits total, encoding two 2-bit selector fields.

============================================================
ENCODING RELATIONSHIP SUMMARY
============================================================

For .tf32 type, each element is 32 bits wide. The two-wide chunk is 64 bits (2 x 32-bit tf32 elements). The sparse operand retains only the non-zero element (32 bits). The metadata uses 2 bits per element group to indicate which of the 2 positions in the chunk holds the non-zero value.

Metadata bit encoding per 2-bit field:
- 00 = element at position 0 (bits [31:0] of chunk) is selected
- 01 = element at position 1 (bits [63:32] of chunk) is selected
- 10 = element at position 2 (used in wider chunks; for 2-wide, encodes position)
- 11 = element at position 3 (used in wider chunks)

In this specific example:
- Lower metadata field [1:0] = 10: selects position 2 within its group
- Upper metadata field [3:2] = 11: selects position 3 within its group

The "Indices of elements within the two-wide chunk" (64-bit field with values 11, 10, 01, 00) shows the full index mapping across all groups, while the actual metadata stored is the compressed 4-bit version (bits 3:0) containing only the two relevant 2-bit selectors (11 and 10) corresponding to the non-zero element positions.


Figure 172 Sparse WGMMA metadata example for `.tf32` type.

The sparsity selector indicates a thread-pair within a group of four consecutive threads which
contributes the sparsity metadata. Hence, the sparsity selector must be either 0 (threads T0, T1) or
1 (threads T2, T3); any other value results in an undefined behavior.

Sparse `wgmma.mma_async.sp` with `.e4m3` and `.e5m2` floating point type
